compounds.name: CJC-1295
aliases: Modified GRF (1-29) [non-DAC variant, commonly sold in research market; distinct molecule from DAC-conjugated CJC-1295]
compounds.beginnerSummary: CJC-1295 is a synthetic 30-amino-acid analog of growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH). In its original Drug Affinity Complex (DAC) form, conjugation to a maleimidopropionic acid linker binds serum albumin and prolongs half-life, producing sustained GH and IGF-1 elevation for roughly a week after a single subcutaneous dose in a small Phase I study. The compound often sold as 'CJC-1295 without DAC' is actually modified GRF (1-29), a shorter-acting and pharmacokinetically distinct GHRH fragment. CJC-1295 has no FDA or EMA approval; modern grey-market supply is the only practical source, and identity, purity, and sterility depend on the vendor.
compounds.researcherDetails: CJC-1295 is a synthetic 30-amino-acid analog of growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH). CJC-1295 has no FDA or EMA approval; modern grey-market supply is the only practical source, and identity, purity, and sterility depend on the vendor.

referenceProfile.reviewSummary: CJC-1295 is a synthetic 30-amino-acid analog of growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH). In its original Drug Affinity Complex (DAC) form, conjugation to a maleimidopropionic acid linker binds serum albumin and prolongs half-life, producing sustained GH and IGF-1 elevation for roughly a week after a single subcutaneous dose in a small Phase I study. The compound often sold as 'CJC-1295 without DAC' is actually modified GRF (1-29), a shorter-acting and pharmacokinetically distinct GHRH fragment. CJC-1295 has no FDA or EMA approval; modern grey-market supply is the only practical source, and identity, purity, and sterility depend on the vendor.
mechanismTargets: GHRH receptor (GHRHR) on anterior pituitary somatotrophs
Prolonged elevation of endogenous GH and IGF-1 in the published Phase I study of the DAC-conjugated form

clinicalEvidence.finding: CJC-1295 is a synthetic 30-amino-acid analog of human growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH), modified to resist enzymatic degradation and, in the DAC form, conjugated to a maleimidopropionic acid linker that binds serum albumin.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Identity verified from the PubChem record and the published Phase I study; sequence and modification details may vary across grey-market preparations.

clinicalEvidence.finding: CJC-1295 binds the pituitary GHRH receptor (GHRHR) on somatotrophs and stimulates endogenous GH synthesis and release; in the DAC-conjugated form, serum albumin binding prolongs circulating half-life and extends the GH/IGF-1 response window.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Mechanism for the DAC form is established by the Alba 2006 Phase I study and the conjugate design literature; the non-DAC variant ('modified GRF (1-29)') does not share this pharmacokinetic profile.

clinicalEvidence.finding: A single subcutaneous dose of DAC-conjugated CJC-1295 produced sustained elevation of GH and IGF-1 over approximately six days in healthy adults and in adults with GHD in a small Phase I study (Alba 2006, JCEM).
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Small Phase I pharmacokinetic study; no Phase III efficacy data and no long-term safety follow-up.

clinicalEvidence.finding: CJC-1295 has no FDA or EMA approval for any indication; the original ConjuChem development program did not result in a marketed product, and modern supply is compounded or research-market.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Regulatory status here is inferred from absence of approval and from the public development record; no specific FDA correspondence is cited for CJC-1295 by name.

clinicalEvidence.finding: CJC-1295 falls under WADA's Section S2 (peptide hormones, growth factors, related substances and mimetics) and is prohibited in- and out-of-competition for athletes subject to the World Anti-Doping Code.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Class-level inclusion under S2; athletes must consult the current year's Prohibited List directly for binding status.

clinicalEvidence.finding: The product commonly sold as 'CJC-1295 without DAC' is modified GRF (1-29), a different molecule from DAC-conjugated CJC-1295 with different pharmacokinetics; treating them as identical in inventory or schedule logs is a known error.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Distinction is widely reported in the published review and pharmacology literature but is not the subject of a single primary comparative trial cited here; users should verify from the product label.

clinicalEvidence.finding: Subcutaneous injection of CJC-1295 carries the generic peptide-injection risk of injection-site reactions (redness, pain, swelling), and compounded or research-market supply adds sterility, identity, and endotoxin risk that depends entirely on the source.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Injection-site reaction is a generic injectable-peptide risk not specific to CJC-1295; compounded sterility and identity risk is a supply-chain observation, not a CJC-1295-specific trial readout.

clinicalEvidence.finding: Sustained elevation of GH and IGF-1 from long-acting GHRH agonism carries theoretical risk of insulin resistance, fluid retention, and effects on pre-existing neoplasia; this has not been established by a CJC-1295-specific long-term trial.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Theoretical extrapolation from the observed pharmacodynamic effect in the Alba 2006 study and from general GH/IGF-1 biology; not directly demonstrated in a CJC-1295 long-term safety cohort.

compounds.name: Gonadorelin
compounds.beginnerSummary: Gonadorelin is the synthetic equivalent of the endogenous hypothalamic decapeptide GnRH (also called LHRH). It binds pituitary GnRH receptors and triggers LH and FSH release. It is approved in the United States as a diagnostic agent for testing pituitary LH/FSH reserve (e.g. historical Factrel). It is also used clinically in pulsatile regimens to treat hypothalamic hypogonadism, including idiopathic hypogonadotropic hypogonadism and hypothalamic amenorrhea, via IV or SC pump delivery. Continuous exposure causes receptor desensitization, so it is not interchangeable with long-acting GnRH superagonists (leuprolide, triptorelin, etc.).

It is also used clinically in pulsatile regimens to treat hypothalamic hypogonadism, including idiopathic hypogonadotropic hypogonadism and hypothalamic amenorrhea, via IV or SC pump delivery. Continuous exposure causes receptor desensitization, so it is not interchangeable with long-acting GnRH superagonists (leuprolide, triptorelin, etc.).

Continuous exposure causes receptor desensitization, so it is not interchangeable with long-acting GnRH superagonists (leuprolide, triptorelin, etc.).
mechanismTargets: GnRH receptor (GnRHR) on anterior pituitary gonadotrophs

clinicalEvidence.finding: Gonadorelin is the synthetic form of native gonadotropin-releasing hormone, a 10-amino-acid hypothalamic decapeptide.
Gonadorelin binds the pituitary GnRH receptor and stimulates LH and FSH release.
Pulsatile GnRH exposure drives physiologic LH and FSH secretion; continuous exposure causes pituitary GnRH-receptor desensitization and downregulation.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Continuous vs pulsatile comparison is established in the literature but is a mechanism claim, not a personal-use protocol.

clinicalEvidence.finding: Intravenous gonadorelin is used as a diagnostic test of pituitary LH and FSH reserve, with serial blood sampling after a single bolus.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Approved diagnostic use refers to labeled products; available products and labeled routes vary by jurisdiction and over time.

clinicalEvidence.finding: Pulsatile gonadorelin delivered by programmable pump (IV or SC) has been used to restore LH, FSH, and sex-steroid secretion and to support ovulation or spermatogenesis in patients with hypothalamic hypogonadism.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Evidence base is small clinical series and case reports; no large modern RCT establishes one universal pump protocol.

clinicalEvidence.finding: Pharmacokinetic studies show that subcutaneous gonadorelin produces delayed and damped plasma GnRH profiles compared with IV bolus, with about one-third of SC dose lost to irreversible clearance during continuous infusion.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Specific pharmacokinetic numbers reflect the cited 1984 and 1987 studies; modern bioequivalence of generic gonadorelin SC products is not separately verified in this record.

clinicalEvidence.finding: Gonadorelin is an FDA-approved diagnostic agent for pituitary LH/FSH reserve testing (historical US brand: Factrel). The historical Factrel brand is no longer marketed; current US supply is typically through compounding pharmacies or non-US labeled products, and the regulatory status of any specific vial must be verified from the actual product label.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Whether any specific user vial is FDA-approved, compounded, or imported must be confirmed from the actual product label; this record does not certify individual products.

clinicalEvidence.finding: Long-term gonadorelin pump therapy has been associated with IgE-mediated hypersensitivity, including local wheal-and-flare injection-site reactions.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Based on a single case report; broader incidence is not quantified in this record.

clinicalEvidence.finding: Subcutaneous or intramuscular injection of gonadorelin carries the generic peptide-injection risks of injection-site reaction, local pain or induration, and sterility risk if reconstitution is poor.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Generic injection-route risk; not a gonadorelin-specific quantified risk in this record.

clinicalEvidence.finding: Native gonadorelin is not the same molecule as the long-acting GnRH superagonists (leuprolide, nafarelin, triptorelin, goserelin, histrelin, buserelin); the superagonists cause sustained receptor activation followed by desensitization, whereas native gonadorelin's effect is route- and schedule-dependent.

safetySignals: subq: Injection-site reactions, local irritation, and pain have been reported with SC administration.

subq: Plasma GnRH profiles are damped with SC delivery compared with IV pulsatile administration; clinical pulsatile protocols using SC routes may require adjusted dosing intervals to maintain a physiologic pulse.

subq: Hypersensitivity reactions including wheal-and-flare at injection sites have been documented with long-term Factrel pump therapy in at least one case report; discontinue and evaluate if hypersensitivity is suspected.
subq: Sterility risk if reconstitution and aseptic technique are inadequate; compounded gonadorelin is not an FDA-approved end-product in most current US supply.

clinicalEvidence.finding: hCG is a 237-amino-acid placental glycoprotein heterodimer composed of an α-subunit shared with LH, FSH, and TSH and a distinct β-subunit that confers biological specificity.
hCG binds and activates the luteinizing hormone / chorionic gonadotropin receptor (LHCGR) on ovarian theca and granulosa cells and on testicular Leydig cells, mimicking LH activity.
A single bolus of hCG (urinary ~5,000–10,000 IU or recombinant 250 µg) administered approximately 34–36 hours before oocyte retrieval is the standard oocyte-maturation trigger in ART cycles.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Specific dosing is not asserted by this record; users should consult the current product label and their clinician.

clinicalEvidence.finding: hCG, alone or in combination with FSH, is an FDA-approved treatment for hypogonadotropic hypogonadism in men and can increase testicular volume and stimulate spermatogenesis.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Specific dosing schedules are not asserted by this record; users should consult the current product label and their clinician.

clinicalEvidence.finding: Multiple hCG products (urinary-derived and recombinant choriogonadotropin alfa) are FDA-approved in the United States for ovulation induction, ART oocyte-maturation trigger, male hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, and pre-treatment of cryptorchidism.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Approvals differ by product; verify the exact indication and route on the specific product label.

clinicalEvidence.finding: hCG administration in women undergoing ART can precipitate ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS); labeling restricts use and triggers clinical monitoring.
clinicalEvidence.limitations: Specific monitoring and prevention strategies are not asserted by this record.

hCG is supplied as an International Unit (IU)-labeled lyophilized or liquid injectable product; final concentration depends on the user-confirmed total IU and diluent volume.
